does: This paper investigates whether transformers use distinct internal mechanisms for recall (retrieving memorized facts) versus reasoning (multi-step inference) through mechanistic interpretability. The authors develop a causal analysis pipeline combining activation patching and structured ablations to probe transformer models at layer, head, and neuron levels using controlled datasets of synthetic linguistic puzzles. Testing on Qwen and LLaMA model families, they identify separable "recall circuits" and "reasoning circuits" within the network, demonstrating that targeted interventions on specific layers and attention heads can selectively impair one ability while preserving the other, with disabling recall circuits reducing fact-retrieval by up to 15% while leaving reasoning intact and vice versa.
connects: This work extends a growing body of mechanistic interpretability research that treats transformers as interpretable computational systems composed of identifiable circuits. It builds on prior work using activation patching, layer-wise analysis, and structured ablations to understand how transformers implement specific capabilities. The paper responds to open questions about the relationship between memorization and generalization in language models, connecting to literature on model generalization, the lottery ticket hypothesis, and debate over whether transformers truly reason or rely on superficial pattern matching. It also engages with recent work on disentangling different types of knowledge representation and capability within neural networks.
enables: This work enables more targeted model evaluation and intervention by providing tools to distinguish which components drive recall versus reasoning. It creates pathways for designing safety-critical interventions that modify one capability without collateral damage to others, relevant for controlling harmful memorization while preserving reasoning. The mechanistic mapping could inform architectural improvements that better separate these capacities or enable selective knowledge updates. It also sets up future work probing whether this circuit separation holds across different model scales, architectures, and training regimes, and whether similar disentanglement exists for other capability pairs. The methodology provides a template for decomposing other compound abilities in transformer models.
